18009:
### Motivation.

## Goal
Propose to add an alternative to enable model parallelism with a single worker, partition model with GSPMD.

## Motivation

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/872501e3-a1bf-45d2-a229-e401236e127b)

_Figure 1: (Left) Current vLLM multi-chip architecture (Right) Proposed single worker architecture_

The current vLLM utilizes multiple workers when a model is partitioned across several devices (**One device per worker**). In this setup, communication operations, such as reducing partial sums or gathering sharded data, are explicitly called within the model's implementation.

In contrast, the PyTorch on TPU stack (PyTorch/XLA and the XLA Compiler) offers an alternative parallelism programming model: Programmer implements models **as if it runs on a single device (No explicit communication op)**, and **provides hints to compiler** on how to partition the model. The compiler then automatically inserts the necessary collective communication operations and applies optimization passes to overlap communication and computation. Therefore, the model code is expected to have **global shape** even if the model is partitioned. The TPU compiler stack is heavily based on this programming model, which was proposed in the [GSPMD](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2105.04663) paper.

Importantly, this model still offers developers the flexibility to explicitly invoke communication operations, allowing them to override the compiler and define a partitioned program manually, similar to vLLM's current programming model.

At the runtime level, all devices within the same host can be managed by a single process, executing the same compiled program. In multi-host, each host has a process managing its local devices.

Here is an [example](https://github.com/lsy323/learning_machine/blob/lsiyuan/torch-xla-example-vllm/torchxla-for-vllm/ffn_spmd.py#L57-L60) to do Megatron style sharding on a FFN module by applying compiler hints using the SPMD API in PyTorch/XLA. For more details on SPMD x PyTorch/XLA, please check this [guide](https://github.com/pytorch/xla/blob/master/docs/source/perf/spmd_basic.md).

## Benefits

1. There are advanced optimizations that can only be enabled through the above compilation pass. Therefore enabling the above programming model in vLLM is essential to the multi-chip inference performance on TPUs.
2. With the new parallel programming model, model code is not coupled with the parallel configuration anymore. This provides vLLM developers with an alternative way to implement model parallelism. (Similar to the programming model in torchtitan, [example](https://github.com/pytorch/torchtitan/blob/508350b7d6f6ca32d48f414852feda5004a7720f/torchtitan/experiments/flux/parallelize_flux.py#L55))

## Combining single worker mode and multi-worker mode

Developers should be able to combine the single worker mode with the multi-worker mode. For example, when pipeline parallelism and tensor parallelism are enabled, each PP stage can have 1 worker, running the TP program on tp_size devices. Since PP is not supported on the TPU backend yet, this use case won’t be covered in this RFC.


### Proposed Change.

## User Configuration

### Use environment variable
We propose to use an environment variable to enable the single worker mode. This may be cleaner because we plan to use single-worker as the default distributed backend for TPU. At that time the new configuration flag is not needed anymore.

## Executor Configuration
In vLLM, different executors are used for single-process and multi-process execution environments. Single-process uses uni executor and multi-process uses mp or ray executor. The executor type is defined in this [field](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/vllm/config.py#L1667-L1668) of the ParallelConfig, and it’s derived from the other parallel configurations (e.g. if TP size is 1, then use uni-process executor, if TP size > 1, then use multi-process executor) [code [ref](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/vllm/config.py#L1753-L1819) for determine executor type].

we can extend the logic of determining distributed executor type to use a single process executor under single worker mode. Here is the code [change](https://github.com/lsy323/vllm/blob/afa57bc83607f398d92a2954ef8f012c07c62802/vllm/config.py#L1808-L1809) in the prototype.

## Runtime configuration
We need to configure runtime to control multiple devices within one process. (By default it’s 1 device / process) This can be done during [init_device](https://github.com/lsy323/vllm/blob/afa57bc83607f398d92a2954ef8f012c07c62802/vllm/v1/worker/tpu_worker.py#L266-L267) with only 1 line change for TPU

## Model initialization / Weight loading

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9601263b-f038-42d8-bccd-5b081e4c5630)
_Figure 2: Current model initialization and weight loading workflow for multi-chip setup_

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/422435b2-f0ec-46d5-a1af-2dbe000a6d1e)
_Figure 3: Model initialization, weight-loading, and model partition for single worker mode_

### Overwrite parallelism configuration
Because we want the model to have global shapes, the model needs to be initialized the same way as (TP=1, EP=1, PP=1). We can tweak the configuration before model loading in [tpu_model_runner.py](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/vllm/v1/worker/tpu_model_runner.py#L889)

### Meta device init, cpu weight loading, transfer to TPU during partitioning

Initializing the full model on CPU may cause host OOM if the model is very large (e.g. DeepSeek v3  - more than 1.2 TB).

To make the model initialization and weight loading more efficient and cleaner, avoid OOM during model init/weight-loading, the proposed workflow will be based on the following:

1. Initialize model on [Meta](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/meta.html) device. Initializing models on TPU has the following drawbacks. Specifically, we need to avoid the following code
```
with target_device:
  model = _initialize_model(vllm_config)
```

  - Drawback 1: Use more memory than the size of the model weights, this leads to OOM even if the size of the model can fit on device. The reason is model initialization logic can have reshape operations, which is not an in-place operation on TPU, requiring additional memory

  - Drawback 2: Initializing the model on TPU would invoke graph compilation and execution, which will significantly slow down the model initialization process.

2. Weight-loading on CPU
If we load the weight on TPU, the drawback is similar to that of doing model initialization on TPU.
  - Drawback 1: Weight loading logic has reshape, view, transpose op, which may not be in-place ops on TPU.
  - Drawback 2: Weight loading on TPU triggers graph compilation and execution, making the weight-loading slow.

Here is the proposed model initialization, weight loading, host->device transfer workflow for TPU (Please see figure 3 for details):

1. Initialize full model on Meta device, which doesn’t allocate physical memory on CPU
2. Add a pre-hook for the load_weight function, which move the weight tensor to CPU
3. Call the load_weight function to load the weight on CPU
4. Add a post-hook to the load_weight to partition the weight (By applying compiler hint) and then transfer to device.

Proposed change:
Add a TPU loader under [vllm/model_executor/model_loader](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/tree/main/vllm/model_executor/model_loader).

## Model Partition

We propose to add a new file [vllm/distributed/tpu_distributed_utils.py](https://github.com/lsy323/vllm/blob/lsiyuan/spmd-merge/vllm/distributed/tpu_distributed_utils.py) to process the loaded model on CPU to:

1. Attach compiler hints for model partitioning 
2. Transfer shards to devices (Implicitly happens when the partitioning hint is applied to weight tensors)
3. Module swap: Replace original model layers with a new layer. For example, we will have the flexibility to replace the FFN module in the model with an optimized kernel in which communication and computation are overlapped.

To achieve this, for each parallel layer involving collective ops in vLLM (e.g. [RowParallelLinear](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/vllm/model_executor/layers/linear.py#L1126), [QKVParallelLinear](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/vllm/model_executor/layers/linear.py#L784)), we implement a function to apply the compiler hints to let the compiler know how the layer is partitioned ([example](https://github.com/lsy323/vllm/blob/afa57bc83607f398d92a2954ef8f012c07c62802/vllm/distributed/tpu_distributed_utils.py#L102-L107)). Then we recursively [process](https://github.com/lsy323/vllm/blob/afa57bc83607f398d92a2954ef8f012c07c62802/vllm/distributed/tpu_distributed_utils.py#L149-L168) the model based on the type of each submodule in the model.

### Why module swap is needed

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3fc09fef-77f8-42bc-815f-c28026ff9e3e)
_Figure 4: QKVParallelLinear with Tensor Parallelism_

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/dc8b9f09-b91c-45c4-b610-850ecd3e88dd)
_Figure 5: Partition the full shape weight of QKVParallelLinear_

For QKVParallel Linear, the `q_proj`, `k_proj` and `v_proj` weights are partitioned along the `num_heads/kv_heads * head_dim` dimension (See figure 4 for details). To simplify the problem, let’s assume `num_kv_heads` is greater than and divisible by `tp_size` here.

However, since the model is initialized with full shape, the `qkv_proj` will be 1 large tensor of shape `[hidden_size, (num_heads + 2 * num_kv_heads) * head_dim]`. Plus when partitioning a tensor with SPMD, the tensor needs to be partitioned evenly along an axis. Partitioning the qkv combined weights will result in an inefficient computational graph (As shown in figure 5).

To make SPMD performant in vLLM, the `q/k/v_proj` weights need to be stored in 3 separate weight tensors separately, so that the weight partition can be...

2. serve the model with `--distributed-executor-backend ray` (without it, vllm engine falls back to V0):
```bash
vllm serve meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct -tp 8 -pp 2 --dtype auto --max-model-len 4096 --gpu-memory-utilization 0.8 --enable-chunked-prefill --distributed-executor-backend ray
```

4. send some GSM8k requests (requires installing `pip install lmeval[api]`)

lm_eval \
  --model local-completions \
  --model_args model="meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct",base_url=""http://localhost:8000/v1/completions"",max_retries=3,timeout=300,tokenized_requests=True,add_bos_token=True,max_length=4096,max_gen_tokens=512,num_concurrent=1 \

  --output_path ${OUTPUT_BASE_PTH}/${CKPT}/${TASK}/${TAG} \
  --show_config
```

18037:
### Motivation.

Snowflake AI Research has recently released several optimizations like Suffix Decoding, LSTM Speculation, Sequence Parallelism, SwiftKV etc, improving TTFT, TPOT and throughput for vLLM via a plugin called Arctic Inference (repo: https://github.com/snowflakedb/arcticinference).

**Performance Improvements**
- 4x faster generation with [Suffix Decoding](https://www.snowflake.com/en/engineering-blog/fast-speculative-decoding-vllm-arctic/) for SWEBench
- 2.4x faster generation with [LSTM Speculator](https://www.snowflake.com/en/engineering-blog/fast-speculative-decoding-vllm-arctic/) for ShareGPT
- 2.8x faster coding with [LSTM Speculator](https://www.snowflake.com/en/engineering-blog/fast-speculative-decoding-vllm-arctic/) for HumanEval
- 2x higher throughput with [SwiftKV](https://www.snowflake.com/en/engineering-blog/swiftkv-llm-compute-reduction/)
- 1.4x throughput than TP=8, but same TTFT as TP=8 with [Arctic Ulysses](https://www.snowflake.com/en/engineering-blog/ulysses-low-latency-llm-inference/)

These optimizations are designed to improve vLLM performance for real production workloads at Snowflake, and will continue to be expanded, maintained, and improved over time

Currently, Arctic Inference is implemented as an external plugin for vLLM. This means users should install both vLLM and Arctic Inference, and then run vLLM with additional configs like:

```
pip install vllm==v0.8.4 arctic-inference

vllm serve ... \
    --sequence-parallel-size ... \
    --speculative-config '{"method": "suffix"}'
```

Problem
--------

Currently, Arctic Inference’s primary user is Snowflake’s own production Cortex inference service. However, we believe that other users in the community can also benefit from its features (there is some interest expressed to us after our most recent speculative decoding blog).

First, how can we make these optimizations more accessible to the vLLM community? While some features (e.g. SwiftKV) are more model-specific, others like Suffix Decoding can provide value to users more generally. We believe more users can benefit if they can use these features through vLLM directly.

Second, how can we build a smooth path for “graduating” features from Arctic Inference to vLLM, if desired, as they become available, more generally used, and as their interfaces stabilize?

### Proposed Change.

Possible Solutions
------------------

1. **All Arctic Inference features remain only available through its plugin.** Basically, keep things the way it is. Arctic Inference features do not get integrated to vLLM directly, and interested users are directed to the Arctic Inference project to install and use it with their installation of vLLM. Of course, users will need to go through extra discovery + installation steps, but it is the simplest. Perhaps Arctic Inference can be highlighted in documentation as an ecosystem project that helps boost performance.

2. **Some Arctic Inference features are directly integrated into vLLM.** This way, vLLM users can benefit from those features without first having to discover the Arctic Inference project (some users asked us about this). The integration can be done in two ways:

    1. Copy the code from Arctic Inference directly to vLLM. Does not require bringing on additional dependencies to vLLM, but will require additional effort to port each feature and maintain them. The Snowflake team will also keep maintaining and improving the same features as part of Arctic Inference, which will make this a duplicated effort. This strategy also does not provide a very smooth “graduation” ramp for features that vLLM might want to integrate in the future.
     
    This also defeats the purpose of supporting [vLLM plugins](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/design/plugin_system.html), which are meant to allow vLLM to leverage optimizations directly without incorporating them into the vLLM’s code base.

    2. Install Arctic Inference as a dependency of vLLM. Then, certain features (e.g. there is interest in Suffix Decoding) can be integrated directly in vLLM, making them immediately available to all vLLM users. Other Arctic Inference features can also be used by optionally enabling its plugin, defaulting to off. The rest of Arctic Inference can even be installed just-in-time, if desired.
     
    There is a question of maintenance burden due to the dependency on Arctic Inference. However, this burden is much lower than other libraries because *Arctic Inference purely exists as an extension to vLLM*, which means:
        1. A part of the Arctic Inference package can be explicitly designated for importing into vLLM, and their interface stability guaranteed (e.g. in a `arctic_inference.core` submodule).
        2. Secondary dependencies are minimal (in fact they are currently non-existent) since Arctic Inference inherits all its major dependencies from vLLM itself, such as torch. For optional features (e.g. those outside `arctic_inference.core`), the Arctic Inference team is responsible for upgrading them after each substantial vLLM release.

Recommended Solution
------------------------

Based on the tradeoff between these three options, we are proposing **option 2.ii**:
- Install Arctic Inference as part of vLLM as a dependency.
- Arctic Inference to create the core submodule, starting with suffix decoding, that has guaranteed interface stability and minimal sub-dependencies.
- The code for integrating suffix decoding with vLLM is contributed to vLLM directly, enabling users to use it natively in vLLM with no extra steps.
- Other features in Arctic Inference can be used on an opt-in basis by enabling the Arctic Inference plugin (off by default).
- Once this integration is established, future features can be smoothly “graduated” into native vLLM usage simply by writing the integration code in vLLM.

18052:
### Motivation.

In many use cases, such as Search, Recommendation, Content Understanding, LLMs are utilized to generate embeddings for downstream use. In these cases, we do not need the sampling steps. 
In V0, pooling/embedding use cases are supported ([doc](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/v0.8.3/models/pooling_models.html)). Currently, when a pooling task is specified, vLLM would [fallback](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/v0.8.5/vllm/engine/arg_utils.py#L1397) to V0 on engine initialization. We propose to add pooling support in V1.

I'd like to seek some early feedback on the ideas as well as implementations in https://github.com/22quinn/vllm/pull/2 before I fully implement it.

### Proposed Change.

### V0 Overview
Key pointers:
* Worker initialize with a pooling model runner: [code](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/v0.8.5/vllm/worker/worker.py#L79)
* encode call adds a request with PoolingParams: [code](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/v0.8.5/vllm/entrypoints/llm.py#L946)
* Pooling model runner executes the model and returns the pooled hidden states: [code](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/v0.8.5/vllm/worker/pooling_model_runner.py#L148)

Key design:
* PoolingModelRunner extends GPUModelRunnerBase
* The normal ModelRunner for text generation also extends GPUModelRunnerBase: [code](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/v0.8.5/vllm/worker/model_runner.py#L1622)

### V1 Proposal
I'm leaning choice 1. 
* Choice 1: Branching based on Pooling/Sampling in model runner and every other places
* Choice 2: Create a separate pooling model runner, like v0

We have a working V1 hack: https://github.com/22quinn/vllm/pull/2 Note this is just a very rough hack to get things working - lots of polish to be done and features to be added.

Major changes needed include:
*Return EngineCoreOutput early for pooling in the main scheduler loop: [code](https://github.com/22quinn/vllm/blob/ffc9034a0bf043025ef7f4bb836a630eb33f3f5a/vllm/v1/core/sched/scheduler.py#L667)
* Return PoolingRequestOutput instead of RequestOutput in the OutputProcessor: [code](https://github.com/22quinn/vllm/blob/ffc9034a0bf043025ef7f4bb836a630eb33f3f5a/vllm/v1/engine/output_processor.py#L344)
* Add hidden states in the ModelRunnerOutput: [code](https://github.com/22quinn/vllm/blob/ffc9034a0bf043025ef7f4bb836a630eb33f3f5a/vllm/v1/worker/gpu_model_runner.py#L1131)
* Some more modifications are needed to skip sampler properly in various places
Another choice is to have a separate PoolingModelRunner just like V0, but it requires some major refactoring of existing model runners to avoid duplicated logic.

### (Optional) Frontend Review

The focus of this RFC is on the V1 backend but I took this opportunity to review the frontend and it seems it could be consolidated, but I might lack some historical context on why we implemented these within vLLM rather than leaving it to the user to further process the embedding.

Currently it supports four types of pooling tasks ([doc](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/v0.8.3/models/pooling_models.html), [code](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/v0.8.5/vllm/config.py#L82)): `embed`, `classify`, `score`, `reward`

For the API call, it supports four method ([code](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/v0.8.5/vllm/entrypoints/llm.py#L873)) on the LLM class: `encode`, `embed`, `classify`, `score`.

Essentially, the only task needed is `embed` and the only API needed is `encode` - everything else is a wrapper and it is possible to eliminate those higher-level features (shifting freedom and responsibility to the user side)

### Feedback Period.

_No response_

18055: ### Your current environment [Collapsed: The output of python collect_env.py] ### 🐛 Describe the bug In vLLM, enabling the prefix‑cache feature in scenarios that trigger recomputation consistently leads to accuracy degradation on GPUs, and the same drops in model accuracy is observed when running the vllm‑ascend fork on NPUs. On GPUs, I launched the vLLM service with the following script, which does not trigger recomputation in this configuration: ```python python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \ --model=/path/to/QwQ-32B \ --trust-remote-code \ --max-model-len 8192 \ -tp 2 \ --port 8006 \ --block-size 128 \ --enable-prefix-caching ``` By contrast, On GPUs, I launched the vLLM service with the following script, which does trigger recomputation by lowering GPU memory utilization to 0.5: ```python python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \ --model=/path/to/QwQ-32B \ --trust-remote-code \ --max-model-len 8192 \ -tp 2 \ --port 8006 \ --block-size 128 \ --enable-prefix-caching \ --gpu-memory-utilization 0.5 ``` With SamplingParams = {"max_tokens": 3072, "temperature": 0}, I measured accuracy on the GSM8K dataset: No recomputation path → 77.79 %, essentially matching the out‑of‑the‑box baseline of 78.39 %. Recomputation path → 60.12 %, nearly 20 percentage points lower than the baseline. Our tests also show a clear trend: the higher the proportion of requests that enter the recomputation path, the more pronounced the accuracy degradation becomes. Under the same configuration on NPUs with vLLM-Ascend, we observed essentially the same behavior. Therefore, based on the evidence above, I suspect that vLLM contains an accuracy bug arising from an adverse interaction between the prefix‑cache feature and the recomputation path. ### Before submitting a new issue... - [x] Make sure you already searched for relevant issues, and asked the chatbot living at the bottom right corner of the [documentation page](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/), which can answer lots of frequently asked questions.

How can we properly disable thinking of Qwen-3 models in the vllm.LLM.chat?
